---
title: "Work package 3. Mexico"
---

Established in 1943, the Mexican Institute for Social Security (IMSS by its acronym in Spanish) is Mexico’s largest public health institution, responsible for delivering medical care, social security benefits, and pension services to formally employed individuals, retirees, and their dependents such as spouses, children, and in some cases, parents and other dependents, who are legally recognized under IMSS regulations. By the end of 2024, IMSS served a total of 77,430,917 beneficiaries which represent 58.5% of the Mexican population (IMSS, 2025). 

A series of databases from IMSS’ Health Information System comprises a comprehensive set of components and modules that document healthcare delivery processes. The system is designed to generate and disseminate statistical information that supports institutional decision-making. Data includes information on morbidity, mortality, births, population demographics, health services coverage, and the availability of human, infrastructural, material, and financial resources. We will use these datasets to implement the open-source software tool for the set-up of the diabetes register.

The absence of comprehensive registries for T1D impedes outcome monitoring, intervention evaluation, and equitable access to specialized care. Addressing these gaps through targeted research and implementation efforts is essential to reduce long-term complications and improve health outcomes among IMSS patients. Thus, a T1D registry would be a strategic asset for IMSS, enabling real-time tracking of clinical outcomes, identification of high-risk individuals, and monitoring of treatment adherence. By integrating sociodemographic and clinical data, IMSS could design targeted interventions, optimize resource allocation, and assess program effectiveness. Furthermore, the registry could inform policy decisions related to disability benefits, preventive care, and endocrinology service planning, shifting the focus from reactive crisis management to proactive, long-term health strategies and cost containment.

<!--ask Karla if she could find a map of Mexico with IMSS clinics-->
